topicArea: Philosophy of Law, Statutory Interpretation & the Limits of Judicial Constraint

questions.passage:
A municipal ordinance prohibits vehicles in the public park. The council that enacted it was, by every surviving account, thinking of automobiles. Years later, a question arrives that no drafter anticipated: does the ordinance forbid an ambulance responding to an emergency? A veterans' group's decommissioned tank, mounted on a pedestal as a memorial? A child's battery-powered toy car? The judge who must answer cannot simply consult the statute, since the statute's words are precisely what generated the question. She must decide what the law means in circumstances its authors never contemplated, and the method she uses to decide is itself the subject of one of the longest-running disputes in legal philosophy.

One school of thought, generally called textualism, holds that a statute means what its enacted words would have conveyed to a reasonably informed reader at the time of enactment, and nothing more. The rationale is partly democratic: only the text was voted on, so only the text carries the authority of enactment, and unwritten purposes or private legislative hopes were never submitted to the process that makes law binding. The rationale is also institutional. Judges asked to enforce words are, on this view, more constrained, more predictable, and less able to substitute their own policy preferences than judges licensed to pursue a statute's deeper aims, since aims are pliable in a way that enacted language is not.

The opposing school, often called purposivism, replies that words alone systematically underdetermine the cases that matter. Statutes are not word puzzles but instruments, enacted to accomplish something, and a faithful interpreter serves the enacting body by asking what the statute was for. Read without purpose, texts generate results their own authors would have regarded as absurd: the ambulance excluded from the park while the memorial tank, being stationary, arguably slips through. Legislatures cannot foresee everything; purposivists argue that treating foreseeability as a condition of coverage quietly converts every drafting gap into a judicial veto over the legislature's evident project.

Textualists have a pointed rejoinder to this appeal to purpose. A legislature is a they, not an it: a body of many members who voted for the same words while wanting different, sometimes incompatible things. The statute's purpose, invoked as though it were a single discoverable fact, is frequently a construction assembled by the interpreter after the fact, from selectively quoted floor statements and committee reports that no majority ever endorsed. A judge authorized to enforce purpose has therefore been authorized, in practice, to select among competing purposes, which restores the very discretion that interpretive method was supposed to discipline, only now concealed behind the vocabulary of fidelity.

Yet textualism, examined with equal severity, faces a symmetrical difficulty. Plain meaning is not a self-executing standard. Words take meaning from context, and the interpreter must choose how much context counts: which dictionary, which linguistic conventions, what level of generality, whether the reasonable reader knows the mischief the statute addressed. The toy car is a vehicle under some dictionaries and not others; the choice between them is not made by the text, because it is a choice about the text. Textualism's promise of constraint depends on treating these upstream choices as if they were not choices, and its critics argue that discretion has not been eliminated but relocated to a stage of the analysis where it is harder to see and therefore harder to contest.

Observers of actual judicial practice have long noted that the two camps behave less differently than their rhetoric suggests. Avowed textualists consult statutory context and evident purpose when the words run out; avowed purposivists begin with the text and treat it as the strongest evidence of purpose. The live dispute, on this reading, concerns not whether text and purpose both matter but which carries the presumption and who bears the burden when they diverge, a dispute about allocating interpretive risk between legislatures and courts rather than about the metaphysics of meaning. Framed this way, the choice of method reflects an underlying judgment about institutional trust: whether one worries more about judges who stray or about legislatures whose words misfire.

What neither camp has produced, after generations of refinement, is a method that eliminates judgment rather than structuring it. Each approach disciplines some discretion while opening room for other discretion; each can be applied well or badly; neither can be operated mechanically by an interpreter with no views at all. That persistent residue suggests the search for a fully constraining method may have mistaken its object. Interpretation of general language by fallible institutions may be the kind of activity in which judgment can be channeled, checked, and made answerable, but not abolished, and a legal culture might be better served by candor about where judgment enters than by methods that promise, without delivering, its disappearance.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Purposivism is the superior interpretive method, since textualism produces absurd results such as excluding ambulances from public parks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Although textualism and purposivism each promise to constrain judicial discretion, each merely relocates rather than eliminates interpretive judgment, suggesting that a method fully abolishing judgment may be unattainable and that candor about where judgment enters would serve legal culture better than promises of its disappearance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because legislatures cannot foresee every future case, statutes should be drafted with greater specificity to prevent interpretive disputes from arising.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Judges who claim to follow enacted text are being deliberately dishonest about the policy preferences that actually drive their decisions.

explanations.A: This choice takes one side of the dispute the passage carefully declines to resolve. The ambulance example is presented as the purposivist's argument against wooden textual reading, but the fourth paragraph gives the textualist rejoinder equal weight, and the passage's conclusion criticizes both camps' claims to have eliminated discretion rather than declaring purposivism superior.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage examines textualism and purposivism in turn, shows that each approach's promise of constraint depends on concealing or relocating discretionary choices rather than removing them, notes that actual practice converges more than the rhetoric admits, and concludes that interpretation may permit judgment to be channeled and made answerable but not abolished, favoring candor over methods that promise judgment's disappearance.
explanations.C: This choice proposes a drafting solution the passage never discusses and that its argument implicitly undercuts. The third paragraph treats the impossibility of foreseeing every case as a structural feature of legislation in general language, not as a defect that more careful drafting could remove, so greater specificity is not presented as a resolution to the dispute.
explanations.D: This choice converts the passage's structural point into an accusation of bad faith. The fifth paragraph argues that textualism relocates discretion to upstream choices where it is harder to see, but the passage attributes this to the nature of interpretation rather than to deliberate dishonesty, and it applies the parallel criticism to purposivism as well.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: concede that textualism, unlike purposivism, succeeds in eliminating judicial discretion through its reliance on dictionaries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: argue that the toy car described in the first paragraph is clearly a vehicle under the ordinance's plain meaning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: show that textualism faces a difficulty symmetrical to the one it identifies in purposivism, since determining plain meaning requires contextual choices that the text itself cannot dictate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: prove that judges should abandon both dictionaries and legislative history when interpreting statutes.

explanations.A: This choice reverses the paragraph's argument. Far from conceding that textualism eliminates discretion, the paragraph argues that reliance on dictionaries illustrates the problem: the choice between competing dictionaries is itself an interpretive decision the text cannot make, so discretion is relocated rather than removed.
explanations.B: This choice mistakes the paragraph's illustrative example for a settled conclusion. The toy car is invoked precisely because it is a vehicle under some dictionaries and not others; the paragraph uses this indeterminacy to show that plain meaning is not self-executing, not to resolve the classification one way.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph opens by announcing a symmetrical difficulty and then develops it: plain meaning requires upstream choices about context, dictionaries, and level of generality that the text does not itself dictate, meaning textualism's promised constraint depends on treating genuine choices as if they were not choices, paralleling the discretion it criticized in purposivism.
explanations.D: This choice attributes a prescriptive recommendation to a paragraph that offers diagnosis rather than reform. The paragraph identifies where discretion hides within textualist method; it does not conclude that judges should abandon dictionaries or any other interpretive resource.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the claim that interpretive method can fully eliminate judicial discretion is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: doubtful, treating the persistent residue of judgment under both major methods as evidence that the goal itself may be misconceived.

choices.label: B
choices.text: confident, since the passage concludes that a properly designed method could eliminate discretion if both camps combined their insights.

choices.label: C
choices.text: indifferent, since the passage regards the question of judicial discretion as unimportant compared to legislative drafting quality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: hostile toward textualism alone, since the passage treats purposivism as having already achieved the elimination of discretion.

explanations.A: Correct. The final paragraph states that neither camp has produced a method that eliminates judgment rather than structuring it, and infers from this persistent residue that the search for a fully constraining method may have mistaken its object, treating interpretation as an activity in which judgment can be channeled and checked but not abolished.
explanations.B: This choice invents a synthesis the passage never proposes. The conclusion does not suggest that combining textualist and purposivist insights would eliminate discretion; it argues the opposite, that the persistence of judgment under both methods indicates elimination may be unattainable in principle.
explanations.C: This choice mistakes the passage's focus. The entire essay is organized around the problem of judicial discretion and how interpretive methods claim to discipline it; legislative drafting quality is never offered as the more important issue, and the passage treats the discretion question as central rather than unimportant.
explanations.D: This choice misses the passage's symmetry. The fourth paragraph's critique of purposivism, that appeals to purpose restore discretion behind a vocabulary of fidelity, is presented with the same force as the fifth paragraph's critique of textualism, so the author's doubt is directed at both camps' claims of constraint, not at textualism alone.

questions.stem: The phrase a they, not an it, as used in the fourth paragraph, most nearly conveys that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: legislatures should be replaced by smaller drafting committees capable of forming unified intentions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: courts and legislatures are separate institutions that should not interfere with one another's functions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: statutes are typically drafted by professional staff rather than by the legislators who vote on them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a legislature is a plural body whose members may vote for identical words while holding differing aims, so a single unified purpose may not exist to be discovered.

explanations.A: This choice converts a descriptive observation into an institutional reform proposal the passage never makes. The phrase diagnoses why appeals to legislative purpose are problematic; it does not recommend replacing legislatures with smaller bodies better suited to forming unified intentions.
explanations.B: This choice reads the phrase as a separation-of-powers principle, which is not its function in the paragraph. The phrase concerns the internal plurality of the legislature itself, not the boundary between judicial and legislative authority.
explanations.C: This choice substitutes a different institutional fact, drafting by staff, for the one the phrase actually describes. The paragraph's point concerns the many members who vote for the same words while wanting different things, not the division of labor between drafters and voters.
explanations.D: Correct. The phrase captures the paragraph's central claim: because a legislature consists of many members who may enact identical language while pursuing different and sometimes incompatible aims, the statute's purpose invoked as a single discoverable fact is frequently the interpreter's own after-the-fact construction rather than something the body collectively possessed.

questions.stem: A statute enacted to reduce industrial river pollution requires permits for any facility that discharges waste into navigable waters. A judge must decide whether the statute covers a facility that injects waste underground, where it migrates through groundwater into a navigable river. The judge rules that it does, reasoning that the statute's evident aim of protecting rivers would be defeated if polluters could evade it by routing identical waste through the ground. Based on the passage, a textualist critic of this ruling would most likely object that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the judge has enforced an aim attributed to the legislature rather than the enacted words, exercising a discretion that the appeal to purpose conceals rather than legitimates.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the judge failed to consult a sufficient number of dictionaries before determining the meaning of the word discharge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the ruling is correct, since textualism requires judges to prevent absurd results that the enacting legislature would have rejected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: groundwater contamination is scientifically indistinguishable from direct discharge, making the case too technical for judicial resolution.

explanations.A: Correct. The judge's reasoning proceeds from the statute's evident aim rather than from what the enacted words convey, which is precisely the move the fourth paragraph's textualist rejoinder targets: purpose invoked as a discoverable fact is often the interpreter's construction, and enforcing it exercises a discretion that the vocabulary of fidelity to legislative aims conceals rather than legitimates.
explanations.B: This choice trivializes the textualist objection into a procedural complaint about research thoroughness. The passage's textualism concerns the authority of enacted words over unenacted purposes, not a requirement to consult any particular number of dictionaries before ruling.
explanations.C: This choice attributes purposivist reasoning to textualism. Preventing results the enacting legislature would have rejected is the purposivist's argument in the third paragraph; the textualist position described in the passage grounds interpretation in what the words conveyed, not in counterfactual legislative reactions.
explanations.D: This choice raises a technical-competence objection foreign to the passage's framework. Neither interpretive camp described in the passage argues that scientifically complex cases fall outside judicial resolution; the dispute concerns which interpretive resources judges may use, not whether they may decide difficult cases.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the passage's claim that avowed textualists and avowed purposivists behave less differently in practice than their rhetoric suggests?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A finding that judges of both schools write longer opinions in statutory cases than in constitutional cases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A finding that most judges cannot articulate the philosophical foundations of their preferred interpretive method when asked.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A large empirical study of statutory decisions finding that self-described textualist judges regularly invoked statutory context and evident purpose when text was indeterminate, while self-described purposivist judges began their analyses with enacted text and treated it as the primary evidence of purpose, producing substantially overlapping patterns of reasoning across the two groups.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A finding that public confidence in courts declines when judges are perceived as ideologically motivated.

explanations.A: This choice measures opinion length rather than interpretive behavior. Longer opinions in statutory cases would reveal nothing about whether textualists and purposivists actually deploy overlapping interpretive resources, which is the specific convergence the sixth paragraph asserts.
explanations.B: This choice concerns judges' ability to articulate philosophical foundations, not their interpretive practice. Judges could be inarticulate about their methods while still behaving very differently from one another, so this finding would not support the claim that the two camps' actual practices converge.
explanations.C: Correct. The sixth paragraph claims that textualists consult context and purpose when words run out while purposivists begin with text and treat it as the strongest evidence of purpose. A large empirical study documenting exactly these crossover behaviors and finding substantially overlapping reasoning patterns would directly confirm that the camps' practices converge despite divergent rhetoric.
explanations.D: This choice concerns public perception of judicial ideology, which does not bear on whether the two interpretive schools' actual reasoning practices resemble each other. Confidence trends are unrelated to the behavioral convergence claim the question asks about.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the passage's claim that no interpretive method can eliminate rather than merely structure judicial judgment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that judges trained in different law schools reach different conclusions in similar statutory cases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Demonstration of a formalized interpretive procedure that, when applied independently by judges holding openly divergent policy views to a large set of previously contested statutory questions, produced identical outcomes in essentially every case without requiring any discretionary choice at any stage of the analysis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that most statutory disputes settle before reaching any judicial decision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that legislatures have increasingly included definitions sections in newly enacted statutes.

explanations.A: This choice supports rather than weakens the passage's claim. Divergent conclusions among similarly situated judges illustrate the persistence of judgment in interpretation, which is exactly the residue the passage says no method has eliminated.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage's claim is that interpretation cannot be operated mechanically by an interpreter with no views, since every method leaves discretionary choices somewhere in the analysis. A demonstrated procedure yielding identical outcomes across judges with openly divergent policy views, on previously contested questions, without any discretionary choice at any stage, would be a direct counterexample to the claim that judgment can only be structured and never eliminated.
explanations.C: This choice concerns how often disputes reach courts, not whether judicial interpretation involves ineliminable judgment when they do. High settlement rates would leave the passage's claim about the nature of interpretation untouched.
explanations.D: This choice describes a drafting trend that might reduce some ambiguities but would not show that interpretive judgment can be eliminated. The passage's argument concerns the structure of interpreting general language, and definitions sections are themselves language requiring interpretation, so their increasing use does not refute the claim.
